question.text: In which of the following scenarios would using options for hedging be PREFERRED over futures?

answer.text: When the hedger wants downside protection but also wants to benefit if the market moves favorably — such as an exporter who wants protection against currency decline but also wants to gain if the currency appreciates
When the hedger wants the cheapest possible hedge with no upfront cost
When the hedger wants to lock in a specific price with certainty
When the hedger wants symmetric risk-reward profile from the hedge

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Options provide asymmetric protection: the put buyer has a floor on losses (strike - premium) but unlimited upside. The exporter buying puts on the foreign currency is protected if it depreciates but gains if it appreciates. Futures lock in a fixed rate (symmetric), eliminating both risk and opportunity. **The Trap:** Futures have no upfront cost (only margin), so they're cheaper than options which require premium. But options are preferred when the hedger wants to retain upside. **Cross-Exam Context:** All three series compare options vs futures hedging. Series IV explicitly states this at the end of Section 5.2.4 — participants can use options to hedge while retaining upside. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Choose your hedge instrument based on your risk tolerance: Futures for certainty at zero premium. Options for flexibility at premium cost. Many corporates use a mix of both.
